sections.paragraph:
, rarely יָכֹול 2Ch 7:7 32:14 fut. יוּכַל (properly, fut. Hophal, to be made able, see Lehrg. page 460; for that this is not fut. Kal, as formerly was thought, and is still repeated, is clear from the fact, that the pr.n. יוּכַל Jer 38:1, is also spelled יְהוּבַל 37:3 ), pl. יוּכְלוּ, יֻֽכְלוּ, inf. const. יְכֹלֶת.

(1) to be able, can. (A cognate root is בּוּל to take, to hold, to contain, to sustain, faffen, tragen können, comp. letter a.) Const. followed by an acc. Job 42:2 more frequently followed by a gerund (Germ. vermögen zu), Gen 13:6, 16 Gen 13:16 45:1 , Gen 45:3 Exo 7:21, 24 by a naked inf. Exo 2:3 18:23 also by a finite verb, Est 8:6, אֵיכָכָה אוּבַל זְרָאִיתִי “how shall I be able to see,” etc. Specially it is-(a) to be able to bear (comp. כּוּל), Isa 1:13 Psa 101:5 more fully יָכֹל לָשֵׂאת Jer 44:22 Pro 30:21 or הָכִיל Amo 7:10.-(b) to be able to bring oneself to do anything. Gen 37:4, “they could not (bring themselves to speak) friendly to him.” Job 4:2. Hos 8:5, ellipt. עַד־מָתַי לֹא יוּעכלָוּ נִקָּיֹן “how long will not they be able (to practise) innocency?” i.e. are they not able to resolve to act uprightly?-(c) to be able lawfully, i.e. to be lawful, or permitted to any one. Gen 43:32, “the Egyptians could not eat with the Hebrews,” i.e. they could not lawfully, it was not permitted to them. Num 9:6 Deu 12:17.

(2) to be powerful, to prevail, whether in fighting or in anything else, Hos 12:5 Jer 3:5 20:7 1Ki 22:22. Followed by לְ of the pers. to prevail over any one in fighting, Gen 32:29. With a verbal suffix (whether it be taken as a dative or an accus.), Jer 20:10 Psa 13:5. With a dative of the thing, metaph. to be master of anything difficult, i.e. to comprehend it, Psa 139:6.

Derived proper names, יְכָלְיָה, יוּכַל, יְהוּכַל.
